topic: An employee discovers the company is being sold
raw_output:
[story 1]

The hum of the server room always sounded like a low, predatory growl to me. I kept glancing at the security cameras, even when I knew they were just recording the usual Tuesday morning routine. Every misplaced file, every late-night email, felt like a beacon flashing for the vultures circling the company. My hands were clammy as I typed up the quarterly report, the keyboard keys feeling slick beneath my fingertips. A shadow seemed to lengthen across the cubicle walls as I reviewed the acquisition prospectus—the language was too slick, too eager. I started triple-checking my login credentials, even though I hadn't accessed anything suspicious. If they were moving, I needed to know what I was protecting, or perhaps, what I was about to lose.

[story 2]

It started with the oddly specific meeting invites. "Strategic realignment," they called it. But the agenda was always vague, the attendees rotating in a way that made my stomach clench. I found myself scrutinizing every interaction in the breakroom, cataloging who laughed too loudly, whose phone calls were unusually hushed. I kept re-reading the company's mission statement, searching for a hidden clause, a coded warning. When Mark from HR mentioned the new leadership team, his smile didn't quite reach his eyes. A cold knot tightened in my chest, a physical pressure that made breathing shallow. I started locking my office door twice a day, the deadbolt clicking home with an excessive, almost frantic finality.

[story 3]

The documents arrived via encrypted channel late one evening, disguised as routine maintenance updates. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat in the quiet office. I read the jargon, the aggressive valuation figures, and a sudden, icy realization washed over me. It wasn't a rumor; it was a blueprint. I spent the next hour pacing the length of my desk, my gaze darting toward the window, convinced someone was watching from the street below. Every casual conversation felt loaded, every shared glance an assessment. I began hoarding my personal files, moving them to obscure drives, a desperate attempt to build a small, impenetrable fortress around what remained of my work, terrified that the walls of the building were already compromised.
